train_pairs.target: I came and saw the man and the dog.
You(sg) sing and I get up.
He saw the house and I took the pig.
She will get up and will take the bread.
We will go away and will sing.
They eat the bread and drink the water.

train_pairs.source: Anon ye weng wengambaranuuwa awon ye weng wengambaraneen.
train_pairs.target: We will hear the dog and he will hear the pig.
I drank the water and she saw the fish.
He takes the bread and eats it.

They will hear the dog and will get up.
I will see the fish and you(sg) will see theman.

verification.hypothesis:
I'm not sure about the translation of the sentence "Om benu aneen." It appears to be in a language that I'm not familiar with. However, I can try to break it down and provide a possible interpretation.

"Om" is a common prefix in many Indian languages, including Sanskrit and Hindi, which means "I" or "me." "Benu" could be a variant of "bana," which means "to do" or "to make." "Anee" could be a variant of "aane," which means "to come" or "to arrive."

With this in mind, a possible translation of the sentence "Om benu aneen" could be:

"I am coming to do something" or "I am making my way to do something."

However, please note that this is just a rough interpretation, and the actual meaning of the sentence may be different depending on the context and the language it is spoken in.

The dog and the pig will hear each other. → Wengambaranuuwa awon wengambaraneen awon.

They will hear the dog and will get up.
I will see the fish and you(sg) will see theman.
He sees the water and I drink it.

best_hypotheses: They eat the bread and drink the water. → Oma oka aniib.
We will hear the dog and he will hear the pig. → Anon ye weng wengambaranuuwa awon ye weng wengambaraneen.
I drank the water and she saw the fish. → Ok anaana oon wedmeen.
The dog will hear the pig and the pig will hear the dog. → Wengambaranuuwa awon wengambaraneen awon.
The man will get up and the woman will get up. → Anon om wengambaranuuwa awon om wengambaraneen.
The dog and the pig will hear each other. → Wengambaranuuwa awon wengambaraneen awon.
